refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable the operator (on-site or remote) to execute earthmoving tasks with measurable productivity of ≥ productivity_target (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations over the agreed evaluation period, while maintaining compliance with worksite safety plan and operational policies.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-001 is a top-level KPI requirement that spans multiple system domains: vehicle capability, operator interface (local or teleoperated), task execution, and safety compliance. Refinement clarifies that productivity is a system-level outcome dependent on vehicle performance, operator effectiveness (including tele-operation capability), and safety-compliant task execution. The requirement is inherently multi-domain and requires decomposition into vehicle performance, operator control, and safety-gated task execution.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure actual m³/h or ton/h throughput during representative worksite operations (e.g., excavation, loading, hauling cycles) over a defined evaluation period (e.g., 8-hour shift). Compare against customer-defined productivity_target. Verify that all measurements comply with worksite safety plan and operational policies (no unsafe shortcuts counted toward productivity).
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-001.1: The vehicle shall deliver sufficient power, speed, and cycle time to support target productivity rates.
FR-NGHE-001.2: The operator interface (local or remote) shall enable precise, responsive task control with minimal latency or cognitive load.
FR-NGHE-001.3: The safety system shall enforce operational constraints (speed limits, geofencing, load limits) without preventing achievement of productivity targets.
FR-NGHE-001.4: The teleoperation system (if deployed) shall maintain operator situational awareness and control responsiveness sufficient for productive task execution.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Integrate high-performance hydraulic drivetrain with optimized cycle-time bucket/blade geometry and operator cab ergonomics to maximize throughput per unit time.

DP-B: Implement a modular task execution framework where vehicle subsystems (propulsion, hydraulics, bucket control) are coordinated via a real-time control layer that accepts operator commands (local or remote) and enforces safety constraints before actuation.

DP-C: Deploy a hybrid approach: vehicle hardware optimized for power/speed, combined with an intelligent task scheduler that sequences operations (dig, swing, dump, return) to minimize idle time and maximize utilization, with safety gates applied at each transition.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Implement a modular task execution framework where vehicle subsystems (propulsion, hydraulics, bucket control) are coordinated via a real-time control layer that accepts operator commands (local or teleoperated) and enforces safety constraints before actuation.

potential_sources: Coupling between operator interface responsiveness and vehicle actuation latency: mitigated by real-time control layer with deterministic scheduling.
Coupling between safety constraint enforcement and productivity: mitigated by designing safety gates as non-blocking filters (e.g., speed limiting rather than task blocking).
Coupling between local and remote operator modes: mitigated by abstracting operator commands to a common interface (Tasking) that both local and teleoperated paths feed.
coupling_check.notes: DP-B decouples the productivity requirement from specific hardware choices, allowing vehicle performance tuning (DP-A) and teleoperation capability (DP-D) to be addressed in parallel sub-decompositions. Safety constraints are enforced at the control layer, not by blocking tasks, preserving productivity potential.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Real-time control layer can be implemented with latency ≤ 100 ms to maintain operator responsiveness.
Assumption: Safety constraints can be expressed as continuous limits (speed, load, geofence) rather than discrete task blocks.

Risk: If safety constraints are overly restrictive (e.g., speed limit too low), productivity target may become unachievable; mitigation requires early safety-productivity trade-off analysis with Safety Manager and Site Manager.
Risk: Teleoperation latency (network + control) may exceed 100 ms, degrading operator responsiveness; mitigation requires network architecture review and predictive control strategies.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: None at this stage. DP-B is sufficiently abstract to accommodate both local and remote operation, and provides a clear decomposition path for vehicle performance, operator interface, and safety integration.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary that orchestrates vehicle subsystems, operator interface (local or teleoperated), safety services, and telemetry collection to deliver productivity-compliant task execution. It owns the real-time control layer (task sequencing, command routing, safety gate enforcement) and coordinates all sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) via standardized interfaces.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (local or remote) specifying desired task (dig, swing, dump, return) and parameters (bucket angle, swing angle, speed). Abstracts both local cab controls and remote tele-operator inputs.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits real-time vehicle state (position, orientation, bucket load, hydraulic pressure, cycle time, productivity metrics) to monitoring systems and remote operator displays.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Broadcasts safety-critical alerts (geofence breach, load limit exceeded, emergency stop triggered, operator override) to Safety Services and Site Manager.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides communication channel for teleoperation commands (Tasking in), telemetry feedback (Telemetry out), and safety alerts (SafetyAlert out) in remote operation mode.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level integration boundary for the productivity KPI. It owns the real-time control layer, task sequencing logic, and safety gate enforcement. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) cross the boundary. All internal coordination (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) is contained.

Vehicle module owns hardware (drivetrain, hydraulics, bucket) and low-level actuation control, but does not own the real-time task sequencing or safety gate logic. Assigning DP-B to Vehicle alone would diffuse control logic across Vehicle and external orchestration, violating cohesion. Vehicle is a sub-module of NGHE Solution.

Teleoperation module owns remote operator interface and network communication, but does not own vehicle control logic or safety enforcement. Assigning DP-B to Teleoperation alone would create diffusion of task execution logic. Teleoperation is a sub-module of NGHE Solution.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alert generation, but does not own vehicle actuation or task sequencing. Assigning DP-B to Safety Services alone would diffuse control logic. Safety Services is a sub-module of NGHE Solution.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of DP-B because it is the only module with sufficient scope to integrate operator commands (via Tasking), vehicle actuation (via Vehicle sub-module), safety constraints (via Safety Services sub-module), and telemetry feedback (via Telemetry). The real-time control layer and task sequencing logic must reside at the NGHE Solution level to avoid diffusion across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a monolithic integration module if sub-module interfaces are not clearly defined. Mitigation: establish explicit interface contracts (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) between NGHE Solution and sub-modules.

Risk: Real-time control layer latency may be difficult to guarantee if NGHE Solution is implemented as a distributed system (e.g., multiple microservices). Mitigation: allocate real-time control logic to a single, deterministic execution context (e.g., vehicle ECU or dedicated real-time OS).

Risk: Safety gate enforcement at NGHE Solution level may create a single point of failure. Mitigation: implement redundant safety monitoring in Safety Services sub-module with independent emergency stop capability.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the internal architecture of NGHE Solution: specify whether the real-time control layer is implemented as a single monolithic component (e.g., vehicle ECU) or as a distributed system (e.g., vehicle ECU + remote control server). This will inform the next level of decomposition and module allocation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (modular task execution framework with real-time control layer) directly addresses FR-NGHE-001 (productivity KPI) by enabling operator commands to be translated into coordinated vehicle actuation while enforcing safety constraints. The DP is sufficiently abstract to accommodate both local and remote operation, and provides a clear decomposition path for vehicle performance, operator interface, and safety integration. No coupling issues identified.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: If real-time control logic is split between Vehicle and NGHE Solution modules, latency and synchronization issues may arise. Mitigation: allocate all real-time control logic to NGHE Solution; Vehicle owns only low-level actuation drivers.

Diffusion risk: If safety gate enforcement is split between NGHE Solution and Safety Services, conflicting constraints may be applied. Mitigation: Safety Services provides constraint definitions; NGHE Solution enforces them in the real-time control layer.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for DP-B. It owns the real-time control layer, task sequencing, and safety gate enforcement. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are sub-modules that provide specialized capabilities (actuation, remote interface, safety policy) but do not own the core control logic. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are clearly defined and do not diffuse across module boundaries.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-001 (productivity KPI) is feasible via field testing: measure m³/h or ton/h throughput during representative operations and compare against productivity_target. Verification of DP-B (real-time control layer) is feasible via simulation and bench testing: validate that operator commands are correctly translated to vehicle actuation with latency ≤ 100 ms and that safety constraints are enforced without blocking productive tasks. Verification of IM (NGHE Solution module) is feasible via integration testing: validate that all sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) correctly interface via Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns the real-time control layer and task sequencing logic. Vehicle owns hardware and low-level actuation. Teleoperation owns remote operator interface. Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement. Each module has a distinct responsibility and clear external interfaces.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-B to specify the real-time control layer architecture: is it implemented as a single vehicle ECU, a distributed system with vehicle ECU + remote server, or a hybrid? This will inform the next level of decomposition.

Define latency and responsiveness requirements for the real-time control layer (e.g., ≤ 100 ms end-to-end latency from operator command to vehicle actuation). This will enable verification of DP-B and inform design trade-offs.
Establish interface contracts for Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link. Specify message formats, timing, and error handling to ensure correct integration of sub-modules.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-001 is a top-level KPI requirement that spans multiple system domains (vehicle, operator interface, safety). The refined FR and selected DP are sound and pass all gates. However, the requirement is not atomic and requires further decomposition into sub-FRs (vehicle performance, operator control, safety-gated execution, teleoperation capability) to enable detailed design and verification. The NGHE Solution IM boundary is appropriate for DP-B, but the internal architecture (real-time control layer, sub-module interfaces) requires further refinement at the next decomposition level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-001.1: The vehicle shall deliver sufficient power, speed, and cycle time to support target productivity rates (e.g., bucket fill time ≤ 30 s, swing time ≤ 10 s, travel speed ≥ 5 km/h).
FR-NGHE-001.2: The operator interface (local or remote) shall enable precise, responsive task control with end-to-end latency ≤ 100 ms and intuitive command mapping.
FR-NGHE-001.3: The safety system shall enforce operational constraints (speed limits, geofencing, load limits) without preventing achievement of productivity targets.
FR-NGHE-001.4: The teleoperation system (if deployed) shall maintain operator situational awareness via real-time telemetry and control responsiveness sufficient for productive task execution.

assumptions: Assumption: Productivity target is defined by customer and is achievable with representative NGHE hardware and operator skill levels.
Assumption: Real-time control layer can be implemented with latency ≤ 100 ms to maintain operator responsiveness.
Assumption: Safety constraints can be expressed as continuous limits (speed, load, geofence) rather than discrete task blocks.
Assumption: Teleoperation network link provides sufficient bandwidth and reliability for remote operation (latency ≤ 200 ms, packet loss ≤ 1%).
Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are known and can be encoded as safety constraints in the control layer.
open_risks: Risk: Safety-productivity trade-off may be unresolvable if safety constraints are overly restrictive. Mitigation: early trade-off analysis with Safety Manager and Site Manager.
Risk: Teleoperation latency may exceed 100 ms, degrading operator responsiveness. Mitigation: network architecture review and predictive control strategies.
Risk: Real-time control layer may become a bottleneck if implemented as a single monolithic component. Mitigation: distributed architecture with deterministic synchronization.
Risk: Operator skill and training may significantly impact productivity. Mitigation: operator training program and performance monitoring.
evidence_needed: Evidence: Customer-defined productivity_target (m³/h or ton/h) and evaluation period.
Evidence: Worksite safety plan and operational policies that constrain vehicle operation.
Evidence: Representative operations data (cycle times, load sizes, terrain) to validate productivity assumptions.
Evidence: Network latency and reliability specifications for teleoperation (if deployed).
Evidence: Operator skill levels and training requirements for local and remote operation.
